text: Nothing fancy here. This is a forty-eight-year-old female with symptomatic cholelithiasis.
The surgeon retracts tissue in a standard manner, likely using robotic instruments to provide a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon operates using four robotic arms: Arm one with a ProGrasp, Arm two with a Maryland dissector, Arm three holding the camera, and Arm four equipped with hook cautery.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and expose the critical view of safety during the cholecystectomy procedure.

An aberrant artery is visible running laterally, just to the right of the hook cautery. The surgeon leaves the artery intact, allowing it to extend up to the cystic plate.
The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety, with the posterior wall of the gallbladder visible against the right hepatic lobe.
The surgeon uses round-tip scissors for dissection, opting for this tool over monopolar scissors.
So I usually use round-tip scissors. Saves about, I think, three hundred dollars for the case, something along those lines.
The surgeon uses a blunt dissection technique to peel the gallbladder off the cystic plate, revealing the underlying tissue layers in a straightforward manner.
